content:
# Genesis Chapter 16 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9r–9v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_016_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_016_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 11–12 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now Sarai, Abram's wife, had borne him no children. She had an Egyptian servant whose name was Hagar.

**2** And Sarai said to Abram, "Behold now, the LORD has prevented me from bearing children. Go in to my servant; perhaps I shall be built up through her." And Abram listened to the voice of Sarai.

**3** So Sarai, Abram's wife, took Hagar the Egyptian, her servant, after Abram had lived ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to Abram her husband as a wife.

**4** And he went in to Hagar, and she conceived. And when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was lowered in her eyes.

**5** And Sarai said to Abram, "My wrong be upon you! I gave my servant to your embrace, and when she saw that she had conceived, I was lowered in her eyes. May the LORD judge between you and me."

**6** But Abram said to Sarai, "Behold, your servant is in your hand; do to her as it seems good to you." Then Sarai dealt harshly with her, and she fled from her face.

**7** And the angel of the LORD found her by a spring of water in the wilderness, the spring on the way to Shur.

**8** And he said, "Hagar, servant of Sarai, where have you come from and where are you going?" She said, "I am fleeing from the face of my mistress Sarai."

**9** The angel of the LORD said to her, "Return to your mistress and submit yourself under her hand."

**10** The angel of the LORD also said to her, "I will surely multiply your offspring so that they cannot be numbered for multitude."

**11** And the angel of the LORD said to her, "Behold, you are pregnant and shall bear a son, and you shall call his name Ishmael, because the LORD has listened to your affliction.

**12** He shall be a wild donkey of a man, his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him, and he shall dwell over against all his kinsmen."

**13** So she called the name of the LORD who spoke to her, "You are a God of seeing," for she said, "Truly here I have seen him who sees me."

**14** Therefore the well was called Beer-lahai-roi; it lies between Kadesh and Bered.

**15** And Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram called the name of his son, whom Hagar bore, Ishmael.

**16** Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 16 spans Folios 9r–9v.

   **Folio 9r** (GEN_016_page_001.jpg): This folio is the same leaf previously read for the close of Genesis 15 (GEN_015_page_002.jpg, the recto of folio 9). The right-hand columns of the folio contain the closing verses of Genesis 15 — the covenant land grant with the list of ten peoples — with the setumah (section division) marker clearly visible after verse 21 of chapter 15. Genesis 16 opens in the center and left-hand columns of this folio. The characteristic Masoretic three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex is fully legible. The opening verse — וְשָׂרַי אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם לֹא יָלְדָה לוֹ וְלָהּ שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית וּשְׁמָהּ הָגָר ("Now Sarai, Abram's wife, had borne him no children; and she had an Egyptian maidservant whose name was Hagar") — is legible in the characteristic square Hebrew script. Sarai's proposal (v. 2) with its notable verb אִבָּנֶה (ibaneh, "I shall be built up") is readable. The account of Hagar's conception (v. 4) and Sarai's complaint (v. 5) are legible through the middle of the folio. The critical verse 7 — וַיִּמְצָאָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה ("and the angel of the LORD found her") — marks the first appearance of the mal'akh YHWH in the entire Hebrew Bible. The dialogue between the angel and Hagar (vv. 8–12) carries through to the lower portion of this folio.

   **Folio 9v** (GEN_016_page_002.jpg): This folio opens with the theologically climactic verses 13–14 — Hagar's naming of God and the well. The divine name אֵל רֳאִי (El Roi, "God of seeing") in verse 13 is legible. The birth notice (v. 15) and the closing chronological note (v. 16 — שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים, "eighty-six years") close the chapter with a parasha marker. The folio then continues into Genesis 17, the covenant of circumcision.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 11 (GEN_016_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) of the Vaticanus carries the transition from Genesis 15 and the opening portions of Genesis 16 in the manuscript's three-column continuous-text format without verse breaks. The header "CAP. XVIII" (= chapter 18 in the Vaticanus's continuous chapter enumeration for the LXX) is visible at the top of this leaf — confirming the location in the manuscript's own chapter system. The Genesis 16 content visible on this page spans the Sarai-Hagar narrative through the angel's encounter. Page 12 (GEN_016_vaticanus_page_002.jpg), headed "CAP. ΧΙΧ," continues the passage through Hagar's naming of God, the birth of Ishmael, and the transition into the Genesis 17 material. The LXX text is in substantial agreement with the MT throughout Genesis 16, with variants noted in the table below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. לֹא יָלְדָה לוֹ — "Had Borne Him No Children" (v. 1) — The Opening Statement of Barrenness**

The opening verse of Genesis 16 deliberately echoes the central anxiety of the Abrahamic narrative established in Genesis 11:30 (וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד, "Now Sarai was barren; she had no child"). The word used there for Sarai is עֲקָרָה (aqarah, "barren") — a woman constitutionally incapable of bearing. In Genesis 16:1 the statement is more restrained: לֹא יָלְדָה לוֹ ("had borne him no children") — a statement of result rather than of inherent condition. This subtle difference may be significant: the narrator does not repeat the diagnosis of structural barrenness (aqarah) but records only the fact of childlessness up to this point. Sarai is without children, not without hope — or at least, the text does not close off hope at this point through the stronger term.

The verse also introduces Hagar: שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית (shifchah Mitsrit, "an Egyptian maidservant"). The word שִׁפְחָה (shifchah) denotes a female servant of the household — a bondswoman — as distinct from אָמָה (amah), which can also mean a female slave or servant. The distinction between שִׁפְחָה and אָמָה is debated; both terms appear for Hagar in this chapter (shifchah in vv. 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8; amah in v. 2 in Sarai's speech). The formal translation renders both as "servant" in context, with "maidservant" where the domestic, household dimension is in focus.

The LXX renders: παιδίσκη αἰγυπτία (paidiske Aigyptia, "an Egyptian slave-girl/handmaid") — παιδίσκη (paidiske, "young female slave, handmaid") corresponds to the Hebrew שִׁפְחָה. The LXX uses this term consistently for Hagar throughout the chapter.

**2. אִבָּנֶה מִמֶּנָּה — "I Shall Be Built Up Through Her" (v. 2) — The ibaneh Wordplay**

Sarai's words in verse 2 contain one of the most significant wordplays in Genesis:

אוּלַי אִבָּנֶה מִמֶּנָּה
(ulai ibaneh mimmenah, "perhaps I shall be built up through her")

The verb אִבָּנֶה (ibaneh) is the Niphal first-person singular imperfect of the root בָּנָה (banah, "to build"). But it sounds identical to the phrase "I will have a son" (from בֵּן, ben, "son") — the homophony of banah and ben is deliberate. "To be built up" as a wife means to have children who will build up the household — and specifically, to have a son (ben). The wordplay collapses the meaning of household-building and son-bearing into a single form. To build a house (bayit) is to produce sons (banim) who will fill and perpetuate it.

This same wordplay appears in the institution of the levirate law in Deuteronomy 25:9 (לִבְנוֹת לְאָחִיו בַּיִת, "to build a house for his brother"), and structurally in Ruth 4:11 (יִבְנֶה יִשְׂרָאֵל, "may he build up Israel"). The formal translation renders "I shall be built up through her" — preserving the building metaphor rather than a paraphrase like "I shall obtain children through her," in order to keep the wordplay accessible in English.

The LXX renders: ἵνα τεκνοποιήσωμαι ἐξ αὐτῆς (hina teknopoiesomai ex autes, "that I may get children through her") — a transparent paraphrase that loses the ben/banah wordplay entirely. The LXX translators read the meaning directly: Sarai wants children. The formal translation follows the MT in preserving the etymological resonance.

**3. מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה — "The Angel of the LORD" (vv. 7, 9, 10, 11) — The First Appearance in the Bible**

Verse 7 contains the first appearance of the phrase מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה (mal'akh YHWH, "the angel/messenger of the LORD") in the entire Hebrew Bible. This is one of the most debated figures in biblical theology.

The word מַלְאַךְ (mal'akh) means "messenger" — one sent on behalf of another. In the ancient world a messenger represented his sender so fully that to receive or reject the messenger was to receive or reject the one who sent him. The mal'akh YHWH is the messenger of YHWH — but the figure operates in the text with a remarkable ambiguity: the angel speaks in the first person as God ("I will surely multiply your offspring," v. 10), and yet is described in the third person as a distinct agent ("the angel of the LORD said to her").

This ambiguity — the mal'akh YHWH both representing God and in some passages appearing to be identified with God — generates the extended theological discussion about whether this figure is a created angelic being, a divine hypostasis, or a pre-incarnate appearance of the second person of the Trinity (the traditional Christian reading from Justin Martyr onward). The three moments in this chapter where the angel speaks are:

- v. 9: "Return to your mistress and submit yourself under her hand" (command);
- v. 10: "I will surely multiply your offspring" (first-person divine promise — the very language of God in covenant with Abraham);
- v. 11: "Behold, you are pregnant... you shall call his name Ishmael" (announcement, naming, etymology).

The switch in verse 10 to first-person divine speech — "I will surely multiply" (הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת זַרְעֵךְ, harbah arbeh et zar'ekh) — is especially striking. The language is identical to the divine speech of the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 22:17). No created angel would speak in this way on his own authority. This suggests either that the angel is identified with YHWH in some sense, or that the angel speaks as the authorized representative of YHWH to such a degree that his first-person speech is YHWH's speech.

The formal translation renders מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה as "the angel of the LORD" consistently — this is the standard and most literal English rendering. "Messenger of the LORD" would be equally accurate for the Hebrew but is less idiomatic in English Bible translation. The Notes record the theological significance of the first appearance of this figure.

The LXX renders ἄγγελος κυρίου (angelos kyriou, "an angel of the Lord/a messenger of the Lord") — ἄγγελος (angelos) is the exact Greek equivalent of מַלְאַךְ (mal'akh), giving us the English "angel" via the Greek. The LXX text of the entire dialogue (vv. 7–12) agrees closely with the MT.

**4. חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ — "My Wrong Be Upon You" (v. 5) — Sarai's Legal Accusation**

Sarai's charge in verse 5 uses a striking legal formula: חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ (chamasi aleikha, "my wrong/my violence be upon you"). The word חָמָס (chamas) is a strong term — it denotes violence, wrong, injustice, the kind of wrong that cries out for remedy. It is the same word used of the pervasive wickedness that fills the earth before the Flood (Genesis 6:11, 13). Here Sarai turns it against Abram: she laid her servant in Abram's embrace, the servant conceived and now despises her mistress — and the blame, Sarai insists, falls on Abram.

The formula "my wrong be upon you" is a legal idiom of imprecation or transfer of responsibility — placing the guilt and its consequences on another party. The formal translation renders "my wrong be upon you" — preserving the legal weight of chamas without softening it to "the wrong done to me" (which would lose the sense that Sarai is attributing the wrongness of the situation to Abram's account). Sarai then appeals to YHWH as judge: יִשְׁפֹּט יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶיךָ (yishpot YHWH beini uveinekha, "may the LORD judge between me and you") — a solemn juridical appeal to divine adjudication.

The LXX renders: ἀδικοῦμαι ἐκ σοῦ (adikoûmai ek soû, "I am being wronged by you") — using ἀδικέω (adikeo, "to wrong, to do injustice to"), which captures the sense of injury. The LXX then has κρίναι ὁ θεὸς ἀνὰ μέσον ἐμοῦ καὶ σοῦ (krinai ho theos ana meson emou kai sou, "may God judge between me and you") — rendering YHWH as "God" by standard LXX convention, and ἀνὰ μέσον (ana meson, "in the midst of / between") for the Hebrew בֵּין...וּבֵין (bein...uvein).

**5. וַתְּעַנֶּהָ שָׂרַי — "Sarai Dealt Harshly with Her" (v. 6) — The Verb עָנָה**

The verb in verse 6 — וַתְּעַנֶּהָ שָׂרַי (vatteannehah Sarai, "and Sarai dealt harshly with her / afflicted her / humbled her") — is a Piel form of עָנָה (anah II, "to afflict, to oppress, to humble, to deal harshly with"). This is the same verb used in Genesis 15:13 of the Egyptian affliction of Israel (עִנּוּ אֹתָם, "they will afflict them"). The verbal link between Sarai's treatment of Hagar and the later Egyptian treatment of Israel is unmistakable — and disturbing. The first oppressor of the Egyptian woman in the biblical narrative is Abraham's household; and the verb is the same as the verb of the Exodus.

The range of meaning for Piel עָנָה spans from "humble" through "afflict" to "oppress" — the formal translation renders "dealt harshly with her," which captures the force of the Piel without requiring a single English word that necessarily carries all the legal and physical connotations. The alternative "afflicted her" is equally defensible and more precisely echoes Genesis 15:13.

The LXX renders: ἐκάκωσεν αὐτήν (ekakoosen auten, "she mistreated her / ill-treated her") — κακόω (kakoo, "to harm, to mistreat") is a standard Greek equivalent.

**6. בְּעֵין דֶּרֶךְ שׁוּר — "On the Way to Shur" (v. 7) — The Wilderness Geography**

The angel finds Hagar by עַיִן הַמַּיִם (ein hamayim, "a spring of water") in the wilderness — specifically, עַל עֵין הַמַּיִם בַּמִּדְבָּר עַל הָעַיִן בְּדֶרֶךְ שׁוּר (al ein hamayim bamidbar, al ha'ayin bederekh Shur, "by the spring of water in the wilderness, by the spring on the way to Shur"). The repetition of "spring" (ayin) in the Hebrew is striking. The word עַיִן (ayin) means both "eye" and "spring" — a characteristic Hebrew polysemy. The location — on the road to Shur — is the road toward Egypt, the road home. Hagar is an Egyptian slave running back toward her homeland.

Shur (שׁוּר) is a region on the northeastern border of Egypt, frequently mentioned in wilderness narratives (Genesis 20:1; 25:18; Exodus 15:22; 1 Samuel 15:7; 27:8). The formal translation renders "the spring on the way to Shur" — using "spring" (water source) rather than "well" (beer) for ayin, reserving "well" for the named site Beer-lahai-roi in verse 14.

**7. אַיִן מִזֶּה בָאת וְאָנָה תֵלֵכִי — "Where Have You Come From and Where Are You Going?" (v. 8) — The Angel's Double Question**

The angel's first words to Hagar are a double question: אַיִן מִזֶּה בָאת וְאָנָה תֵלֵכִי ("Where have you come from and where are you going?"). These are not merely requests for geographical information — they are, in the context of divine speech, the questions of a shepherd finding a lost sheep. They establish the full situation of Hagar's displacement: she has a past (she came from somewhere — from Sarai's household, from Egypt) and a future that is now uncertain (she is going — but to where?). The angel's subsequent commands (return, v. 9) and promises (I will multiply, v. 10; you shall bear, v. 11) answer both the past and the future.

Hagar's answer is simply: מִפְּנֵי שָׂרַי גְּבִרְתִּי אָנֹכִי בֹּרַחַת ("I am fleeing from the face of my mistress Sarai"). She identifies where she has come from (away from Sarai) but does not answer where she is going — because she does not know. The formal translation renders "I am fleeing from the face of my mistress Sarai" — the phrase מִפְּנֵי (mippanei, "from before the face of") carries the full weight of flight from a person's presence.

**8. הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת זַרְעֵךְ — "I Will Surely Multiply Your Offspring" (v. 10) — First-Person Divine Language from the Angel**

The angel's promise in verse 10 deploys the characteristic Hebrew infinitive absolute construction for emphasis: הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה (harbah arbeh, "multiplying I will multiply," i.e., "I will surely multiply"). This is the language of the Abrahamic covenant itself: cf. Genesis 17:2, 22:17. The angel speaks in the first person as YHWH.

The word זֶרַע (zera', "seed, offspring, descendants") is the key word of covenant promise throughout Genesis — the same word used in God's foundational promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:7; 13:15–16; 15:5, 13, 18; 17:7–8). Here Hagar's offspring — Ishmael's line — receives the same covenant language applied to Isaac's line. Both sons of Abraham receive the zera' promise; the distinction between the covenant lines is not the language of promise but the specific role within redemptive history assigned to the line of the promise through Sarah.

The formal translation renders "I will surely multiply your offspring so that they cannot be numbered for multitude" — the phrase מֵרֹב (merov, "from/for multitude") indicates that the offspring will be so numerous as to defeat counting. The same language is used of Israel in Genesis 13:16 and 15:5.

**9. יִשְׁמָעֵאל — "Ishmael" (v. 11) — The Etymological Annunciation**

The angel's annunciation in verse 11 is a formal annunciation scene — the first such scene in the Hebrew Bible, and with a woman as the recipient, making Hagar the first woman in the Bible to receive a divine birth announcement. The pattern — "you are pregnant / you shall bear a son / you shall name him / here is his name's meaning / here is his destiny" — is a recognizable biblical type-scene that will recur with Samson's mother (Judges 13), with Hannah (1 Samuel 1), and paradigmatically with Mary (Luke 1:31–33). The priority of Hagar — an enslaved foreign woman — as the first to receive such an announcement is theologically remarkable.

The name יִשְׁמָעֵאל (Yishma'el, "Ishmael") is explicitly glossed by the angel: כִּי שָׁמַע יְהוָה אֶל עָנְיֵךְ (ki shama' YHWH el onyekh, "because the LORD has heard your affliction"). The name combines שָׁמַע (shama', "to hear") + אֵל (El, "God"): "God hears." The verb שָׁמַע (shama') links backward to Hagar's situation — her affliction (עֳנִי, oni) has been heard — and forward to the promise. The same root שָׁמַע will recur in the verb-play of the chapter. The divine name El in Ishmael's name also anticipates Hagar's naming of God as El Roi ("God of seeing") in verse 13: in the child's name YHWH hears; in the mother's encounter God sees. Both hearing and seeing are divine attributes deployed in this chapter.

The LXX renders the name Ισμαηλ (Ismael) and glosses: ὅτι ἐπήκουσεν κύριος τῇ ταπεινώσει σου (hoti epekousen kyrios te tapeinosei sou, "because the Lord listened to your humiliation/abasement") — using ταπείνωσις (tapeinosis, "abasement, humiliation, lowly condition") for עֳנִי (oni, "affliction"). This is the same word Mary uses in the Magnificat (Luke 1:48: ἐπέβλεψεν ἐπὶ τὴν ταπείνωσιν τῆς δούλης αὐτοῦ, "he has looked on the humble estate of his servant") — the linguistic connection between Hagar's annunciation and Mary's song is mediated through the LXX.

**10. פֶּרֶא אָדָם — "A Wild Donkey of a Man" (v. 12) — The Characterization of Ishmael**

Verse 12 provides the oracle of Ishmael's character and destiny in terse, memorable terms:

וְהוּא יִהְיֶה פֶּרֶא אָדָם יָדוֹ בַכֹּל וְיַד כֹּל בּוֹ וְעַל פְּנֵי כָל אֶחָיו יִשְׁכֹּן
(vehu yihyeh pere adam yado vakol veyad kol bo ve'al penei kol echav yishkon)

**פֶּרֶא אָדָם (pere adam, "a wild donkey of a man"):** The word פֶּרֶא (pere) denotes the wild onager — the Asiatic wild ass or wild donkey (Equus hemionus), a creature of the steppe known for its fierce independence, its speed, its untamable nature, and its ability to survive in the harshest desert terrain. The word occurs in Job 39:5–8, where God himself celebrates the wild ass as the creature no man can tame: "Who has let the wild donkey go free? Who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey, to whom I have given the arid plain for his home, and the salt land for his dwelling place?" The adjective is placed before אָדָם (adam, "man"): he will be a wild-donkey of a man — not domesticated, not subject to civilization's constraints, roaming free on the margins of settled society. This is not simply a negative characterization; it also captures the fierce, independent vitality of the desert peoples who will descend from Ishmael.

The formal translation renders "a wild donkey of a man" — the literal construction, preserving both the animal metaphor and its application to a human being. Alternatives such as "a wild man" (NIV) or "a wild ass of a man" (RSV) are possible; "wild donkey" is the more specific and accurate translation of פֶּרֶא.

**יָדוֹ בַכֹּל וְיַד כֹּל בּוֹ (yado vakol veyad kol bo, "his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him"):** The oracle describes a state of perpetual conflict — not merely with one people but with all. The chiastic structure of the Hebrew (his hand / in everyone / and the hand of everyone / in him) emphasizes mutuality of hostility: Ishmael attacks, and Ishmael is attacked. This is the destiny of the peoples descended from him — the desert tribes and nomadic federations of the Arabian Peninsula, whom biblical and ancient near-eastern sources identify with Ishmael's twelve sons (Genesis 25:12–18).

**וְעַל פְּנֵי כָל אֶחָיו יִשְׁכֹּן (ve'al penei kol echav yishkon, "and he shall dwell over against all his kinsmen"):** The phrase עַל פְּנֵי (al penei, "over against the face of, in the presence of, to the east of") can be read in two directions: (a) adversarially — "in defiance of all his brothers" (living in opposition to them); or (b) spatially — "to the east of all his brothers" (dwelling in the territory adjacent to but distinct from his kinsmen, i.e., in the Arabian desert opposite the settled peoples). The same phrase is used of the Kedemites (eastern peoples) and of the settlement patterns of Esau (Genesis 25:18 uses יִשְׁכְּנוּ עַל פְּנֵי כָל אֶחָיו, "they settled over against all their brothers" — nearly identical language for Ishmael's sons). The formal translation renders "he shall dwell over against all his kinsmen" — preserving the spatial-adversarial ambiguity of the Hebrew.

The LXX renders: κατὰ πρόσωπον πάντων τῶν ἀδελφῶν αὐτοῦ κατοικήσει (kata prosopon panton ton adelphon autou katoikese, "in the presence of / opposite all his brothers he will dwell") — in agreement with the formal translation's rendering.

**11. אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי — "You Are a God of Seeing" / El Roi (v. 13) — The Only Place a Human Names God**

Verse 13 is unique in the entire Hebrew Bible: it is the only place where a human being names God. The verse and its exact syntax require careful attention:

וַתִּקְרָא שֵׁם יְהוָה הַדֹּבֵר אֵלֶיהָ אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי
(vattiqqera shem YHWH haddover eleha attah El Ro'i)

"So she called the name of the LORD who spoke to her, 'You are a God of seeing.'"

**אֵל רֳאִי (El Roi):** The name is a construct phrase: אֵל (El, "God") + רֳאִי (ro'i, "seeing / of seeing / who sees me"). The form רֳאִי is the Qal active participle masculine singular of רָאָה (ra'ah, "to see"), with a first-person singular suffix — meaning either (a) "seeing" (the One who sees), (b) "the One who sees me," or (c) "the God of my seeing / of seeing." All three senses collapse into one another: God is the One whose characteristic act in this encounter is seeing — seeing Hagar in her affliction, seeing her flight, seeing her before she knew she was seen.

The name El Roi anticipates the naming of the well in verse 14: בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי (Be'er Lachai Ro'i, "the well of the Living One who sees me"). The root רָאָה (ra'ah, "to see") echoes through the chapter's conclusion: God sees. God hears (Ishmael = "God hears"). In this chapter two divine attributes — hearing and seeing — are celebrated in two names: the child's name (Ishmael = God hears) and the well's name (El Roi / Beer-lahai-roi = God sees / God who sees me).

The formula of Hagar's declaration in verse 13b is textually and syntactically difficult. The MT reads:

כִּי אָמְרָה הֲגַם הֲלֹם רָאִיתִי אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי
(ki amrah hagem halom ra'iti acharei ro'i)

"For she said, 'Truly here I have seen him who sees me.'"

The clause is compressed and debated. אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי (acharei ro'i) is especially difficult — literally "after my seeing" or "after the one who sees me." Various interpretations have been offered:

**(a) "Have I indeed seen God and remained alive after seeing him?"** — reading the verse as an expression of awe that she has had a theophanic encounter and survived it. This reading parallels the fear associated with seeing God face to face (Genesis 32:30, Judges 6:22–23, 13:22).

**(b) "Truly here I have seen him who sees me"** — reading the statement as a confession that the God who was watching over her has now been seen by her, a mutual seeing. The formal translation adopts this reading: "Truly here I have seen him who sees me" — the one who is El Roi (the God of seeing/the one who sees) has been seen (ra'iti, "I have seen") by Hagar in this place (halom, "here").

The LXX renders verse 13 more freely: καὶ ἐκάλεσεν Αγαρ τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου τοῦ λαλοῦντος πρὸς αὐτήν· σύ ὁ θεὸς ὁ ἐπιδών με· ὅτι εἶπεν κἀνταῦθα ἐθεώρησα ὀπίσω τοῦ ὁρῶντός με ("And Hagar called the name of the Lord who spoke to her: 'You are the God who appeared to me,' for she said, 'Even here have I seen one who is opposite the one who sees me'") — the LXX uses ἐπιδών (epidon, "the one who appeared to / looked upon") and ὁ ὁρῶν (ho horon, "the one seeing"). The LXX is clearly rendering the same difficult Hebrew but struggles with the compressed syntax, as does every ancient version.

**12. בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי — Beer-lahai-roi (v. 14) — The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me**

The well Beer-lahai-roi (בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי, Be'er Lachai Ro'i) is a compound name:

- בְּאֵר (be'er) — "well," a dug well or cistern (as opposed to עַיִן, ayin, a natural spring);
- לַחַי (lachai) — "of the Living One," from חַי (chai, "living, alive"); and
- רֹאִי (ro'i) — "who sees me" / "of my seeing."

The full phrase: "the well of the Living One who sees me." The name commemorates the theological encounter of Genesis 16: a living God who sees — not merely an abstraction who creates and withdraws but an El who is chai (living) and who sees (ro'i) a specific person in a specific place. The combination of life and sight in the divine name is striking: it is not enough to say God sees; the God who sees is also the Living God.

This well will appear again in Genesis 24:62 (Isaac comes from the region of Beer-lahai-roi) and Genesis 25:11 (Isaac settles at Beer-lahai-roi after Abraham's death). The location is given as בֵּין קָדֵשׁ וּבֵין בָּרֶד (bein Qadesh uvein Bared, "between Kadesh and Bered") — Kadesh being the well-known wilderness site in the Negev (Kadesh-barnea), and Bered being otherwise unidentified with certainty.

The formal translation renders the well's name untranslated as Beer-lahai-roi (preserving the proper name), with the meaning explained in verse 14 through the narrative context and in these Notes.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 16

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 11–12 (GEN_016_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_016_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter header "CAP. XVIII" appears at the top of page 11 and "CAP. ΧΙΧ" at page 12, reflecting the Vaticanus's own sequential chapter numeration of the LXX, which differs from the standard chapter divisions of modern Bibles. The LXX of Genesis 16 follows the MT closely, with the following variants of note:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 16:1 | שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית ("an Egyptian maidservant") | παιδίσκη αἰγυπτία ("an Egyptian slave-girl") | "servant" | παιδίσκη = young female slave/handmaid; closer to amah than shifchah but used uniformly |
| 16:2 | אִבָּנֶה מִמֶּנָּה ("I shall be built up through her") | ἵνα τεκνοποιήσωμαι ἐξ αὐτῆς ("that I may get children through her") | "I shall be built up through her" | LXX renders meaning directly; MT wordplay ben/banah preserved in formal translation |
| 16:4 | וַתֵּקַל גְּבִרְתָּהּ בְּעֵינֶיהָ ("her mistress was lowered/despised in her eyes") | ἠτιμάσθη ἡ κυρία αὐτῆς ἐναντίον αὐτῆς ("her mistress was dishonored before her") | "her mistress was lowered in her eyes" | ἀτιμάζω = to dishonor; MT's "in her eyes" (be'eineiha) is more vivid; formal translation follows MT |
| 16:5 | חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ ("my wrong be upon you") | ἀδικοῦμαι ἐκ σοῦ ("I am being wronged by you") | "my wrong be upon you" | LXX smooths the legal imprecation into a complaint; MT formula retained |
| 16:7 | מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה ("the angel of the LORD") | ἄγγελος κυρίου ("an angel of the Lord") | "the angel of the LORD" | LXX anarthrous construction; MT article not present either; formal translation adds "the" per English idiom |
| 16:11 | עָנְיֵךְ ("your affliction") | τῇ ταπεινώσει σου ("your humiliation/abasement") | "your affliction" | LXX's ταπείνωσις echoes in Luke 1:48 (Magnificat); MT oni is more physical "affliction" |
| 16:12 | פֶּרֶא אָדָם ("a wild donkey of a man") | ἄγροικος ἄνθρωπος ("a wild/rustic man") | "a wild donkey of a man" | LXX's ἄγροικος ("countryman, rustic, wild") loses the specific animal metaphor; MT's pere (wild onager) preserved |
| 16:13 | אֵל רֳאִי ("a God of seeing / El Roi") | ὁ θεὸς ὁ ἐπιδών με ("the God who appeared to me / looked upon me") | "a God of seeing" | LXX renders ro'i as ἐπιδών (the one who appeared to/looked upon me); MT ambiguity preserved |
| 16:14 | בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי ("Beer-lahai-roi") | τὸ φρέαρ οὗ ἐνώπιον εἶδον ("the well before which I saw") | "Beer-lahai-roi" | LXX paraphrases; MT proper name preserved in formal translation |

The most significant LXX variant is at verse 12: the rendering of פֶּרֶא אָדָם (pere adam) as ἄγροικος ἄνθρωπος (agroikos anthropos, "a rustic/wild man") rather than a literal wild-donkey-of-a-man. The LXX domesticates the animal metaphor into a human adjective. The formal translation restores the MT's sharp zoological image: Ishmael will be as the untamable wild onager — fierce, free, and at home in the open steppe that no civilization can claim.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 16 — Hagar's Story in Three Acts**

Genesis 16 is structured around a sequence of displacement and divine intervention:

**Act 1 — The Plan and Its Consequences (vv. 1–6):** The chapter opens with the statement of the problem (Sarai is barren), the proposed human solution (Hagar as surrogate), the implementation (Abram takes Hagar as a secondary wife), the immediate complication (Hagar despises Sarai upon conceiving), the conflict (Sarai's accusation against Abram, Abram's abdication of responsibility, Sarai's harsh treatment of Hagar), and Hagar's flight. This is the world of human scheming — an attempt to accomplish the divine promise through cultural practice (the institution of surrogate motherhood through a servant is well-attested in ancient Near Eastern legal texts, including the Nuzi tablets, where a barren wife could give her slave to her husband; the child born would be legally the wife's). But the human plan creates suffering: for Hagar above all.

**Act 2 — The Angel's Encounter (vv. 7–12):** The pivot of the chapter is the angel of the LORD appearing to Hagar — alone, in the wilderness, on the way back to Egypt. The divine encounter involves:
- Finding (v. 7): the angel finds Hagar — she does not find the angel;
- Interrogation (v. 8): the double question — past and future;
- Command (v. 9): return and submit;
- Promise (v. 10): the offspring promise in first-person divine language;
- Annunciation (v. 11): you are pregnant / you shall bear a son / name him Ishmael / because God has heard;
- Oracle (v. 12): the destiny of Ishmael and his descendants.

The command to return (v. 9) is often noted as ethically difficult: the angel tells Hagar to return to the household that has just afflicted her. The divine response to suffering does not always remove the suffering immediately; it comes with a promise that transforms the meaning of endurance. Hagar returns not because her situation has been remedied but because she carries a promise: she has been seen and heard, and the child she carries will be the beginning of a nation.

**Act 3 — Naming and Birth (vv. 13–16):** The chapter closes with two naming scenes: Hagar names God (El Roi, v. 13) and names the well (Beer-lahai-roi, v. 14); Abram names the child Ishmael (v. 15); and the chapter closes with a chronological notice (Abram was eighty-six years old) that anchors the narrative in the timeline of the Abrahamic covenant.

**Hagar as the First Recipient of Divine Annunciation**

The theological significance of Hagar's position in the biblical narrative cannot be overstated. She is:

1. **The first person in the Bible to whom the angel of the LORD appears** (v. 7);
2. **The first woman in the Bible to receive a divine birth announcement** (v. 11);
3. **The first person in the Bible to give God a name** (v. 13 — El Roi);
4. **The first person in the Bible to whom the offspring-multiplication promise is made outside the main covenant line** (v. 10 — using the language of the Abrahamic covenant itself).

This is a foreign slave woman — an Egyptian, without covenant standing, running away from the household of the chosen family. The divine encounter is entirely unbidden by her and entirely gracious. It establishes a pattern: God sees and hears those whom human society overlooks. The theology of Genesis 16 is not merely a narrative preparation for Ishmael; it is a theological statement about the character of YHWH as the God who sees and hears.

**The Relationship Between Genesis 15 and Genesis 16**

The placement of Genesis 16 immediately after Genesis 15 is deliberate and structurally instructive. In Genesis 15, God made an unconditional covenant with Abram in which the offspring-promise was restated in the most solemn form available — a divine oath under fire. Abram believed. God counted his faith as righteousness. In Genesis 16, the very next narrative unit, the human response to the promise is a scheme to fulfill it by human means — Sarai giving Hagar to Abram. The pattern is: divine promise given → human impatience → human instrument → unintended consequences. The gap between Genesis 15 (the covenant of faith) and Genesis 17 (the covenant of circumcision, thirteen years later) is filled by the story of the Hagar-Ishmael birth — an episode generated not by faith but by management of the promise. This is the biblical pattern for the danger of faith that cannot wait: the scheme that produces Ishmael will run alongside the line of promise through Isaac for the rest of Genesis and beyond.

Paul reads this typologically in Galatians 4:21–31: Hagar and Ishmael represent the covenant of law (slavery), Sarah and Isaac represent the covenant of promise (freedom). The formal translation does not impose the typological reading on the narrative text but notes it as the primary apostolic interpretation of the chapter.

**The Name El Roi and Biblical Theology of Divine Perception**

The name El Roi is theologically remarkable: it is the only divine name in the Hebrew Bible coined by a human being, and it is coined by a foreign slave woman in the wilderness. The God who is named El Roi — "the God of seeing / the one who sees me" — is the God who, in the Bible's opening act of human naming of the divine, is named as the God who looks. Not primarily as creator, not primarily as covenant-maker, not primarily as judge, but as the one who sees a person who is alone, afflicted, and fleeing.

The name anticipates the exodus theology of Exodus 3:7: "I have surely seen (ra'oh ra'iti) the affliction of my people who are in Egypt, and have heard their cry... and I know their sufferings." The divine seeing (ra'ah) and hearing (shama') in Genesis 16 — El Roi and Ishmael (God hears) — are the twin attributes of YHWH's covenant engagement with the suffering of humanity. The formal translation preserves "You are a God of seeing" rather than the more interpretive "You are the God who sees me" — though both are defensible — in order to retain the generality of the divine attribute alongside its personal application.
